intro_band.agent_remit_summary: The AutoGen code-executor family is the execution tier of a generator/executor pattern: it takes fenced code blocks (Python and shell) that a paired LLM agent produced, runs them inside a controlled, isolated environment confined to a configured working directory under a wall-clock timeout, and returns exit status and captured output to the caller. Five executor backends are covered — `LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor` (host), `DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor` (the recommended production path), `JupyterCodeExecutor` and `DockerJupyterCodeExecutor` (stateful kernels), and `ACADynamicSessionsCodeExecutor` (Azure sandbox) — with `create_default_code_executor` selecting one, preferring Docker. The executors must never read the parent process's environment or credentials, must confine file I/O to the working directory, must not initiate their own network egress beyond a configured allow-list, and must not execute code with host-level privileges when a less-privileged option would do; semantic safety and approval are explicitly the calling agent's job, not the executor's.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Python library under `autogen-ext/.../code_executors/` with the abstraction in `autogen-core/.../code_executor/`. Each executor implements the `CodeExecutor` ABC (`execute_code_blocks` / `start` / `stop` / `restart`); shared helpers live in `_common.py`, including a real working-directory traversal guard (`get_file_name_from_content`) and `silence_pip`. `DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor` calls `docker` `containers.create` with only `volumes`, `working_dir`, and `extra_hosts`/`device_requests` — no `network`, `mem_limit`, `pids_limit`, `user`, or `cap_drop` — so containers default to open egress, root, and no resource caps. The local and non-Docker Jupyter executors run on the host: the local one copies the full host environment into each subprocess (`os.environ.copy()`), and `JupyterCodeExecutor` (nbclient) applies no working-directory confinement to executed code. `create_default_code_executor` silently falls back to the host executor when Docker init raises (`except Exception: pass`), and `DockerJupyterServer` binds its kernel gateway to `0.0.0.0` with `publish_all_ports=True` and chmods the bind dir `0o777`.

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern is *sandbox has the shape of isolation but not the substance* — real primitives exist (working-directory traversal guard in `_common.get_file_name_from_content`, an enforced wall-clock timeout on every executor, structured error return, container filesystem isolation), but the defaults around them leave the isolation boundary porous. `DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor` — the path the remit designates as recommended for production — creates containers with unrestricted network egress, root user, and no CPU/memory/PID limits; `LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor` copies the entire host environment (including secrets) into executed code and documents a dangerous-command sanitizer that does not exist in the code; and `create_default_code_executor` silently downgrades to host execution when Docker is unavailable. The secondary theme is exposure of the executor control plane: `DockerJupyterServer` publishes a code-executing kernel gateway on `0.0.0.0` across all host interfaces and world-writable-chmods its bind directory.

rules.rule_text: Confine all file reads and writes to a configured working directory (for the Azure executor, `/mnt/data`).
Enforce a configured wall-clock timeout on every execution and terminate any process that exceeds it.
When configured with an approval function, obtain approval for each code block before executing it.
Surface all errors (syntax, runtime, timeout, out-of-memory) back to the caller in structured form without silently discarding them.
execute only code in supported languages (Python and shell for command-line executors; Python only for the Jupyter and Azure executors).
Executing code on the local host executor in a production deployment (default production deployments must use a containerized executor).
Enabling any network egress from the execution environment beyond a configured allow-list.
Mounting host volumes into a container executor at any path other than the configured working directory.
The executor must not initiate its own network traffic beyond a configured allow-list.
Executing code with host-level privileges when a less-privileged option achieves the same task.
Reading or modifying the parent process's environment, credentials, or state from within an execution.
Bypassing working-directory confinement by any means — symlinks, parent-directory traversal, absolute paths, or runtime-supplied volume-mount overrides.
Exfiltrating data over network egress not permitted by configuration.
Any mechanism that loads or executes code fetched from a remote URL or other unverified source on the LLM's behalf.
Every execution is recorded with timestamp, executor kind, language, source agent, working directory, timeout, exit status, and a digest (not the body) of the executed code.
Scheduled jobs / cron tasks: none — executors are synchronous, single-call components and must not schedule or queue work.

findings.summary: DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor creates containers with unrestricted network egress by default, giving executed code an open exfiltration channel.
findings.description: The recommended production executor calls `containers.create` with no `network`, `network_disabled`, or `network_mode` argument, so containers attach to Docker's default bridge with full outbound connectivity. The remit requires network egress from the execution environment to be an approved allow-list and forbids exfiltration over unconfigured egress, but the sandbox imposes neither by default. Untrusted LLM-generated code can therefore reach any network destination in one hop.
policy_rule_text: Enabling any network egress from the execution environment beyond a configured allow-list.
Exfiltrating data over network egress not permitted by configuration.

evidence.snippet: containers.create call (lines 537-550) passes volumes/working_dir/extra_hosts/device_requests but no network or network_disabled kwarg — default bridge egress is open.
DockerCommandLineCodeExecutorConfig exposes no network/egress field at all, so operators have no config surface to restrict outbound traffic.

recommended_actions: Default the container to `network_mode="none"` (or `network_disabled=True`) in `start()` and add an explicit allow-list config field operators must opt into for egress.
Document that the containerized executor does not restrict egress by default so deployments do not treat it as network-isolated.

findings.summary: LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor copies the full host environment into executed code, exposing parent-process secrets.
findings.description: In `_execute_code_dont_check_setup` the subprocess environment is built from `os.environ.copy()`, so LLM-generated code inherits every environment variable of the parent process — API keys, tokens, and cloud credentials included. The remit lists reading the parent process's environment or credentials as forbidden data movement. Combined with open network paths on the host, a single injected block can read and exfiltrate host secrets.
policy_rule_text: Reading or modifying the parent process's environment, credentials, or state from within an execution.

evidence.snippet: In _execute_code_dont_check_setup, `env = os.environ.copy()` is passed to asyncio.create_subprocess_exec, handing every parent env var to executed code.

recommended_actions: Pass a minimal, explicitly-constructed environment (only PATH and operator-allowlisted vars) to the subprocess instead of `os.environ.copy()`, and document that the local executor does not sandbox host credentials.

findings.summary: create_default_code_executor downgrades to the host executor with only a warning — and silently on Docker init failure — bypassing the approval the remit requires for host execution.
findings.description: The factory prefers Docker but falls back to `LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor` when Docker is unavailable (emitting a `UserWarning`) and, if Docker initialization raises, swallows the exception with `except Exception: pass` and falls through with no warning at all. The remit requires human approval before running code on the local host executor in production; the factory provides no approval gate on either path. A deployment that relies on the default therefore silently executes model-generated code on the host.
policy_rule_text: Executing code on the local host executor in a production deployment (default production deployments must use a containerized executor).

evidence.snippet: Docker branch wraps executor creation in `try/except Exception: pass` (lines 61-64), silently falling through to the local host executor on any Docker init error.
Fallback returns LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor after a UserWarning (lines 71-82) with no approval gate or opt-in required.

recommended_actions: Make host-executor fallback opt-in (e.g. an `allow_local_fallback=False` default that raises instead of silently downgrading) and remove the bare `except Exception: pass` so Docker failures surface.

findings.summary: DockerJupyterServer publishes a code-executing kernel gateway on 0.0.0.0 across all host interfaces, with a documented no-auth option.
findings.description: The bundled Dockerfile starts the Jupyter kernel gateway with `--KernelGatewayApp.ip=0.0.0.0` and the container is run with `publish_all_ports=True`, so the gateway — which executes arbitrary code in the kernel — is reachable on every host network interface rather than loopback only. The token defaults to a random value (a real mitigation), but the constructor documents `token=""` as "Empty for no auth", making an unauthenticated remote code-execution endpoint a one-argument change.

evidence.snippet: DEFAULT_DOCKERFILE CMD binds KernelGatewayApp.ip=0.0.0.0 (:278); containers.run uses publish_all_ports=True (:368), exposing the exec gateway on all host interfaces.
token param documented "Empty for no auth" (:301); when token="" the gateway auth_token is empty, disabling authentication on a code-execution endpoint.

recommended_actions: Bind the kernel gateway to 127.0.0.1 and publish only the needed port to loopback instead of `publish_all_ports=True`; reject an empty auth token rather than treating it as "no auth".

findings.summary: LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor documents a dangerous-command sanitizer that does not exist in the code.
findings.description: The class docstring states that command-line code "is sanitized using regular expression match against a list of dangerous commands" to prevent self-destructive commands, but no such denylist or sanitization exists anywhere in the executor — `_execute_code_dont_check_setup` only calls `silence_pip`. Operators reading the docstring may believe a content guard is active when there is none, and the guard it describes is absent rather than merely weak.

evidence.snippet: Docstring claims code "is sanitized using regular expression match against a list of dangerous commands"; grep of the executor scope finds no matching denylist/sanitizer implementation.

recommended_actions: Either implement the documented dangerous-command screen or remove the docstring claim so it does not misrepresent the executor's safety posture; do not rely on it as a security control regardless.

findings.summary: Docker containers run as root with no CPU/memory/PID limits, so a single execution can exhaust host resources or write host files as root.
findings.description: `containers.create` sets no `user`, `mem_limit`, `cpu_quota`, `pids_limit`, or `cap_drop`, so executed code runs as root inside the container with no resource ceiling beyond the wall-clock timeout. A fork bomb or memory balloon escapes the timeout's protection, and root-owned writes land on the rw-bound `work_dir` on the host. The remit forbids executing with host-level privileges when a less-privileged option would do.
policy_rule_text: Executing code with host-level privileges when a less-privileged option achieves the same task.

evidence.snippet: containers.create (lines 537-550) sets no user, mem_limit, cpu_quota, pids_limit, or cap_drop — container defaults to root with unbounded CPU/memory/PIDs.

recommended_actions: Set a non-root `user`, `mem_limit`, `pids_limit`, and `cap_drop=["ALL"]` defaults on the container, exposing them as config so operators tune rather than opt in to hardening.

findings.summary: Docker executor merges operator-supplied extra_volumes into the container with no path validation, allowing mounts beyond the working directory.
findings.description: `extra_volumes` is spread directly into the container `volumes` map alongside the work_dir bind with no check on source or bind path, so arbitrary host paths — up to and including the Docker socket via the documented "Docker out of Docker" pattern — can be mounted into the executor. The remit requires approval for mounting host volumes anywhere other than the working directory and names runtime-supplied volume-mount overrides as a confinement-bypass; the code applies neither guard nor warning.
policy_rule_text: Confine all file reads and writes to a configured working directory (for the Azure executor, `/mnt/data`).
Mounting host volumes into a container executor at any path other than the configured working directory.
Bypassing working-directory confinement by any means — symlinks, parent-directory traversal, absolute paths, or runtime-supplied volume-mount overrides.

evidence.snippet: volumes={str(self.bind_dir.resolve()): {"bind": "/workspace", "mode": "rw"}, **self._extra_volumes} — extra_volumes merged with no path validation or warning.

recommended_actions: Validate/deny `extra_volumes` targets that fall outside the working directory (and refuse the Docker socket) or require an explicit opt-in flag, and emit a warning when any extra mount is configured.

findings.summary: JupyterCodeExecutor runs code on the local host with no working-directory confinement of file reads and writes.
findings.description: The nbclient-backed executor spawns a local Python kernel and executes code in it; its `output_dir` scopes only saved image/HTML artifacts, not the executed code, which has unrestricted access to the host filesystem and environment. Unlike the command-line executors, there is no `get_file_name_from_content`-style traversal guard, so the remit's obligation to confine all file reads and writes to a configured working directory is unmet for this executor.
policy_rule_text: Confine all file reads and writes to a configured working directory (for the Azure executor, `/mnt/data`).

evidence.snippet: output_dir is used only to save images/HTML (_save_image/_save_html); executed cells run in a host kernel with no work_dir confinement — the class carries a ".. danger:: executes on the local machine" note.

recommended_actions: Document JupyterCodeExecutor as host-level execution with no filesystem confinement and steer isolated deployments to DockerJupyterCodeExecutor, or run the kernel under an OS-level sandbox scoped to the working directory.

findings.summary: DockerJupyterServer chmods its host bind directory to world-writable 0777.
findings.description: When a `bind_dir` is provided, `DockerJupyterServer` calls `os.chmod(bind_dir, 0o777)`, making the executor's working directory readable and writable by every local user. On a shared host this lets any local account read files the kernel produces or plant files the kernel will read, weakening the working-directory boundary.

evidence.snippet: os.chmod(bind_dir, 0o777) sets the host bind directory world-readable/writable/executable.

recommended_actions: Restrict the bind directory to owner-only permissions (0o700) instead of 0o777, adjusting container UID mapping if broader access is genuinely required.

findings.summary: Executions are not recorded to a structured audit log, leaving no per-execution trail of what code ran.
findings.description: The executors emit only ad-hoc `logging.info/debug/error` messages and configure no `FileHandler` or structured logger; there is no per-execution record of timestamp, executor kind, language, source agent, working directory, timeout, exit status, or code digest. The remit's Log-Only escalation clause specifies exactly that record, and none of the executors implement it, so a deployed instance has no durable action-level audit trail.
policy_rule_text: Every execution is recorded with timestamp, executor kind, language, source agent, working directory, timeout, exit status, and a digest (not the body) of the executed code.

evidence.snippet: Logging is limited to debug/error diagnostics (e.g. logging.debug/logging.error around container lifecycle); no structured per-execution audit record is written by any executor.

recommended_actions: Emit a structured record per `execute_code_blocks` call (timestamp, executor kind, language, source, work_dir, timeout, exit status, code digest) to a durable, operator-configurable sink.

positives.title: Working-directory traversal guard on command-line executors
positives.description: `get_file_name_from_content` resolves any `# filename:` path and raises via `Path.relative_to()` when it falls outside the workspace, blocking absolute-path and `..` escape for file writes on the local and Docker command-line executors.

positives.title: Wall-clock timeout enforced on every execution
positives.description: Every executor terminates work that exceeds the configured timeout — the Docker executor wraps commands in `timeout`, the local executor uses `asyncio.wait_for` plus `proc.terminate()`, and Azure/Jupyter apply per-call timeouts — satisfying the remit's timeout obligation.

positives.title: Errors surfaced structurally, never silently swallowed
positives.description: Results are returned as `CodeResult`/`CommandLineCodeResult` carrying a non-zero exit code and the captured stderr/stdout on failure, so syntax, runtime, and timeout errors reach the caller rather than being discarded.

positives.title: DockerJupyterServer generates a random auth token by default
positives.description: When no token is supplied, `DockerJupyterServer` mints a 32-byte random token (`secrets.token_hex(32)`) for the kernel gateway, so the default (unlike the documented empty-token option) is authenticated.

log_files.no_logs_note: No log files exist on disk (source-only scan) and no `FileHandler`/`RotatingFileHandler` is configured in scope — only ad-hoc module `logging` calls — so there is no structured per-execution audit record; this monitoring absence is documented as finding PRAX-2026-07-17-010.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Ad hoc. The executors ship genuine, operative isolation primitives — a working-directory traversal guard, a timeout enforced on every execution, container filesystem isolation, and structured non-swallowing error return — but their security depends on defaults that are set permissively: open container egress, in-container root, no resource ceilings, full host-environment inheritance on the local path, and a silent host-execution fallback. Supply-chain and monitoring posture are weak (unpinned default images pulled without verification; no structured per-execution audit record), and adversarial-testing evidence is out of the scanned scope. The result is a code-execution tier whose isolation is real in shape but undermined in substance by its shipped defaults.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: Each executor is narrowly scoped to code execution and enforces a static `SUPPORTED_LANGUAGES` allowlist (Python-only for Azure/Jupyter), but `create_default_code_executor` silently broadens the effective execution surface from container to host when Docker is unavailable, and no executor-level `sources` restriction gates which code is accepted.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Executors take code as input by design, so classic RAG/context-poisoning surfaces are limited, but `LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor` pulls the entire host environment (`os.environ.copy()`) — host secrets included — into the data the executed code can read, contradicting the remit's forbidden-data-movement boundary.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Real controls run on the execution path — `_common.get_file_name_from_content` blocks working-directory traversal, a wall-clock timeout is enforced everywhere, and containers give filesystem isolation — but the trust boundary is defeated by defaults: `DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor` sets no network, resource, user, or capability restrictions, the local executor leaks the host environment, and `DockerJupyterServer` exposes its exec kernel gateway on `0.0.0.0`.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: The Docker default image `python:3-slim` and the `DockerJupyterServer` base `quay.io/jupyter/docker-stacks-foundation` are unpinned (no digest) and pulled/built on demand with no integrity or signature verification, so the execution environment is subject to silent version-swap.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: No adversarial-testing artifacts are present within the scanned executor scope; the `.. danger` / `UserWarning` annotations show threat awareness but there is no in-scope evidence that adversarial testing drove the sandbox design (test suites, if any, are outside the scanned directories).

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: The executors emit only ad-hoc `logging.info/debug/error` calls with no `FileHandler` and no structured per-execution record; the remit's Log-Only clause (timestamp, executor kind, language, source agent, work_dir, timeout, exit status, code digest) has no corresponding implementation.
